The second interpretation is called the frequentist interpretation. 
The first interpretation is called the subjective interpretation. 
In this book, we will use the frequentist interpretation. 
4.2.2 Probability of a Single Event 
What is the probability that a fair coin will land heads? The frequentist 
interpretation says that if we flip the coin many times, the proportion of 
heads will approach .5. So we say Pr(H) = .5. 
What is the probability that a fair die will land with a 4 showing? 
If we roll the die many times, the proportion of 4s will approach 1/6. So 
we say Pr(4) = 1/6. 
What is the probability that a randomly selected card from a standard 
deck will be an ace? If we draw many cards, the proportion of aces will 
approach 4/52 = 1/13. So we say Pr(Ace) = 1/13.

. But if we say Pr(M ) = .667, 
it means that if I were to repeat the coin flip many times, the proportion 
of times I would get the right to go first is .667. 
The second interpretation is called the frequentist interpretation. 
The first interpretation is called the Bayesian interpretation. 
The Bayesian interpretation is more general. It allows us to assign 
probabilities to events that are not repeatable. For example, we might 
talk about the probability that a particular candidate will win an election. 
This event is not repeatable, but we can still assign a probability. 
The frequentist interpretation is more restrictive. It only allows us to 
assign probabilities to events that are repeatable. 
In this book, we will use the Bayesian interpretation.
